Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. It is one of the most common and serious diseases of apple trees, producing dark, scabby lesions on leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety spots on upper leaf surface
Yellowing and premature leaf drop
Dark, scabby, corky lesions on fruit surface
Cracked or deformed fruit in severe infections
Lesions on young shoots and twigs
treatment: Apply fungicides containing myclobutanil, captan, or mancozeb at bud break
Spray every 7–10 days during wet spring weather
Use systemic fungicides (thiophanate-methyl) for active infections
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves to reduce spore load
Prune trees to improve air circulation and reduce humidity
prevention: Plant scab-resistant apple varieties (Liberty, Freedom, Enterprise)
Rake and dispose of fallen leaves in autumn
Apply dormant lime-sulfur sprays before bud break
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of trees
Monitor weather and apply protective sprays before rain

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It affects fruit, leaves, and bark, causing significant losses in orchards if left unmanaged.
symptoms: Small purple spots on leaves enlarging into brown lesions with purple borders (frog-eye leaf spot)
Fruit rot starting as a small brown spot, enlarging to rot the entire fruit
Infected fruit turns black and mummified on the tree
Reddish-brown cankers on branches and bark
Wilting and dieback of infected limbs
treatment: Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl fungicides during the growing season
Prune and destroy all infected branches, mummified fruit, and cankers
Apply fungicides starting at pink bud stage through cover sprays
Remove dead or dying wood that serves as a source of infection
Use copper-based sprays after pruning to protect wounds
prevention: Prune out fire blight strikes and dead wood regularly
Remove mummified fruit from trees before the growing season
Maintain good orchard sanitation — clear fallen fruit and debris
Avoid wounding trees during cultivation
Ensure proper tree nutrition to reduce stress susceptibility

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae, a fungus requiring both apple and Eastern red cedar (juniper) to complete its life cycle.
symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on the upper surface of apple leaves
Tube-like structures (aecia) on the underside of leaves
Orange lesions on young fruit, causing deformity
Premature leaf drop in severe cases
Affected fruit becomes unmarketable
treatment: Apply myclobutanil, triadimefon, or mancozeb fungicides at pink bud stage
Spray every 7–10 days from pink bud through petal fall
Use systemic triazole fungicides for best curative effect
Remove nearby juniper/red cedar trees if feasible to break the disease cycle
Apply protective sprays before forecasted rain events in spring
prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties (Redfree, William's Pride)
Remove Eastern red cedar trees within radius when possible
Do not plant apples near ornamental junipers
Monitor orange gelatinous galls on junipers in spring as warning sign
Apply preventive fungicide programs starting at green tip stage

Apple___healthy.description: The apple plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease, pests, or stress.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue regular monitoring for early pest and disease detection
Maintain a scheduled fertilization and pruning program
prevention: Apply dormant oil sprays in early spring to control overwintering pests
Maintain proper tree spacing for good air circulation
Practice balanced fertilization — avoid excess nitrogen
Implement an integrated pest management (IPM) program
Remove and dispose of fallen leaves and fruit promptly

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry plant is healthy and free from visible disease symptoms.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue routine monitoring and care
prevention: Maintain soil pH between 4.5 and 5.5 for optimal blueberry health
Mulch around plants to conserve moisture and suppress weeds
Prune annually to remove old canes and improve productivity
Irrigate consistently — blueberries are sensitive to drought
Monitor for blueberry maggot and mummy berry

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherry is caused by Podosphaera clandestina. It thrives in warm, dry conditions and is one of the most recognizable fungal diseases due to its distinctive white powdery coating.
symptoms: White powdery fungal coating on young leaves, shoots, and fruit
Affected leaves curl upward and become distorted
Infected shoots may be stunted
Fruit may show russeting or cracking in severe cases
Premature defoliation in heavy infections
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicides at first sign of infection
Use sterol-inhibiting fungicides (myclobutanil, trifloxystrobin) for systemic control
Spray potassium bicarbonate as an organic alternative
Remove and destroy heavily infected shoots
Apply fungicides every 10–14 days during susceptible growth periods
prevention: Plant powdery mildew-resistant cherry varieties where available
Prune to open the canopy and increase air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes soft, susceptible growth
Begin preventive sprays at bud break in orchards with disease history
Monitor young tissue closely during warm, dry spring weather

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry plant appears healthy with no signs of disease or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue routine orchard management practices
prevention: Apply dormant copper sprays to prevent fungal overwintering
Maintain proper spacing and annual pruning for canopy openness
Monitor for cherry fruit fly and aphid populations
Ensure adequate irrigation especially during fruit development
Avoid late-season nitrogen applications

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.disease_name: Corn Gray Leaf Spot (Cercospora Leaf Spot)
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis and is one of the most yield-limiting diseases of corn worldwide. It thrives under warm, humid conditions with heavy dew.
symptoms: Small, rectangular, tan-to-gray lesions parallel to leaf veins
Lesions restricted by leaf veins, giving a brick-like pattern
Lesions enlarge and turn gray with yellow margins
Premature death of lower leaves in severe infections
Yield loss due to reduced photosynthetic area
treatment: Apply strobilurin fungicides (azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin) at VT to R2 growth stage
Triazole fungicides (propiconazole, tebuconazole) are also effective
Use premix fungicides (strobilurin + triazole) for broadest protection
Time application to protect the ear leaf and above at tasseling
prevention: Plant GLS-resistant or tolerant corn hybrids
Rotate corn with non-host crops like soybeans or wheat
Till corn residue after harvest to reduce inoculum
Avoid fields with poor air drainage and high humidity
Scout fields regularly from V8 stage onward

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust is caused by Puccinia sorghi. It is widespread but rarely causes major yield loss in field corn; sweet corn is more vulnerable.
symptoms: Small, circular to elongated, brick-red to brown pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules rupture to release powdery, cinnamon-brown spores
Heavily infected leaves may turn yellow and die prematurely
Pustules can appear on husks, leaf sheaths, and stalks in severe cases
Significant infections before silking can reduce yields
treatment: Apply triazole fungicides (propiconazole, tebuconazole) at early rust detection
Strobilurin-based fungicides provide good preventive protection
Fungicide applications are most economical on sweet corn or susceptible hybrids
Spray before disease reaches the ear leaf for best results
prevention: Plant rust-resistant corn hybrids
Early planting to avoid peak rust spore seasons
Monitor crops weekly once warm, humid conditions develop
Crop rotation and residue management reduce local inoculum
Scout for rust from V8 stage through early grain fill

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern Leaf Blight (NLB) is caused by Exserohilum turcicum. It is a major foliar disease that can cause significant yield losses when infection occurs before or at tasseling.
symptoms: Large, cigar-shaped, gray-green to tan lesions 2–6 inches long
Lesions may coalesce, killing large portions of the leaf
Dark spores visible in the center of older lesions
Rapid blighting of foliage from lower canopy upward
Severe infections before silking can cause 30–50% yield loss
treatment: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicides at tasseling (VT) growth stage
Premix fungicide products provide best results
A single well-timed application at VT/R1 is usually sufficient
Scout fields from V8 onward and treat when disease is first detected on lower leaves
prevention: Plant NLB-resistant hybrids — most modern commercial hybrids have some resistance
Rotate corn with non-host crops to reduce residue-borne inoculum
Till corn debris after harvest
Avoid continuous corn especially in areas with high disease history
Monitor weather: cool (65–80°F), humid conditions with heavy dews favor NLB

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: The corn plant appears healthy with uniform green color and no signs of disease or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue regular field scouting and crop management
prevention: Use certified, high-quality seed from reputable sources
Practice balanced fertilization based on soil tests
Rotate crops annually to break disease and pest cycles
Monitor for early signs of disease from V6 stage onward
Maintain proper plant population to avoid canopy stress

Grape___Black_rot.description: Black rot of grape is caused by Guignardia bidwellii. It is one of the most devastating grape diseases in humid climates, capable of destroying the entire crop.
symptoms: Reddish-brown circular spots on leaves, often with a dark border
Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in the center of leaf lesions
Infected berries turn brown then shrivel into hard black mummies
Dark brown cankers on shoots and tendrils
Infected clusters may be entirely lost
treatment: Apply myclobutanil, mancozeb, or captan fungicides from bud break through veraison
Spray every 10–14 days, more frequently during wet weather
Remove and destroy all mummified berries and infected shoots during dormant pruning
Use systemic triazole fungicides for active infections
prevention: Prune to open vine canopy for better air circulation and spray penetration
Remove mummies from vines and the ground before bud break
Train vines on trellises to keep fruit off the ground
Apply protective fungicide program from early shoot growth
Avoid wetting foliage when irrigating

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca, also known as Black Measles, is a complex wood disease of grapes caused by a consortium of fungi. It is a chronic, progressive disease with no complete cure.
symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern on leaves — interveinal chlorosis and necrosis
Berries develop dark spots or streaks (measles symptoms) and may crack
Internal wood discoloration — brown/black streaking when branches are cut
Sudden vine collapse (apoplexy) in hot weather
Progressive decline in vine productivity over years
treatment: No fully curative treatment is currently available
Remove and burn severely infected wood during dormant pruning
Apply wound sealants (Trichoderma-based products) after pruning cuts
Use double-pruning technique to delay exposure of pruning wounds to rain
prevention: Make pruning cuts during dry weather to minimize fungal spore entry
Treat large pruning wounds with fungicide-based sealants
Use certified disease-free planting material
Avoid over-pruning which creates large wounds
Delay pruning until late in the dormant season in high-risk areas

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Isariopsis Leaf Spot is caused by Pseudocercospora vitis. It appears late in the season and causes defoliation in susceptible cultivars.
symptoms: Irregular, dark brown to black spots on upper leaf surface
Spots may have yellow halos and merge to form large necrotic areas
Dark, velvety fungal sporulation on the underside of lesions
Premature defoliation weakening vines before harvest
Can affect petioles and young shoots in severe cases
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or mancozeb at disease onset
Strobilurin or triazole fungicides provide systemic control
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves to reduce inoculum
Spray program should cover late summer and early autumn months
prevention: Maintain good canopy management to improve air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply balanced nutrition — avoid excess nitrogen
Include late-season fungicide applications in the spray program
Use resistant or tolerant grape varieties where available

Grape___healthy.description: The grapevine appears healthy with no visible signs of disease, pest damage, or nutritional deficiency.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue routine vineyard monitoring and management
prevention: Implement a season-long fungicide spray schedule as a preventive measure
Prune annually for canopy management and air circulation
Monitor for downy mildew and powdery mildew throughout the season
Maintain balanced soil fertility through annual soil testing
Scout for insect pests such as grape berry moth and leafhoppers

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Huanglongbing (HLB) or Citrus Greening is caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, spread by the Asian citrus psyllid. It is the most destructive citrus disease in the world with no known cure.
symptoms: Asymmetric blotchy mottling (yellowing) of leaves — pattern differs from nutritional deficiency
Yellow shoots with green-veined leaves (opposite to normal patterns)
Small, lopsided, bitter fruit that remains green even when ripe
Fruit drops prematurely
Progressive decline in tree vigor leading to tree death within years
treatment: No cure is currently available for HLB-infected trees
Aggressive control of Asian citrus psyllid using imidacloprid or other systemic insecticides
Remove and destroy infected trees immediately to prevent spread
Nutritional therapy (enhanced fertilization) can extend productive life of infected trees
prevention: Use certified disease-free nursery stock only
Monitor and control Asian citrus psyllid populations with regular scouting
Apply systemic insecticides to control psyllid vectors
Quarantine measures — do not move plant material from infected areas
Report suspected HLB infections to local agricultural authorities immediately

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of peach is caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. It is one of the most widespread and destructive diseases of peaches and nectarines in humid growing regions.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that become angular and purple-brown
Lesions may fall out leaving a shot-hole appearance
Severe leaf yellowing and defoliation in high-pressure years
Fruit develops small, shallow pits or cracks that turn dark and corky
Cankers on young twigs and shoots
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides from shuck split through harvest
Spray oxytetracycline (where registered) for in-season control
Prune out infected twigs during dormancy
Apply fixed copper sprays in fall after leaf drop and in spring before bud swell
prevention: Plant resistant peach varieties (Redhaven, Reliance, Contender)
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation
Prune to open canopy for better air movement and spray coverage
Avoid planting in low-lying areas prone to frost and high humidity
Start copper spray program at bud swell and maintain throughout season

Peach___healthy.description: The peach tree appears healthy with no signs of bacterial, fungal, or viral disease.
treatment: No treatment required
Maintain regular orchard monitoring schedule
prevention: Apply dormant copper sprays before bud break
Thin fruit to improve air circulation and reduce disease pressure
Monitor for peach leaf curl and brown rot throughout the season
Maintain proper tree nutrition — avoid excess nitrogen
Protect against peach borer insects with appropriate treatments

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of pepper is caused by Xanthomonas euvesicatoria. It affects leaves, stems, and fruit, causing significant losses in warm, wet conditions.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that become brown with yellow halos
Lesions may coalesce causing large necrotic areas
Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop
Fruit develops raised, scabby, dark lesions
Infected fruit is unmarketable and prone to secondary rot
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides combined with mancozeb for better efficacy
Spray at first sign of disease and repeat every 5–7 days in wet weather
Remove and destroy heavily infected plant material
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet to prevent spread
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants and treated seeds
Plant resistant or tolerant pepper varieties
Practice crop rotation — avoid planting peppers after tomatoes
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead irrigation
Maintain 2–3 year rotation away from solanaceous crops

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The bell pepper plant appears healthy with no signs of disease, pest damage, or nutritional deficiency.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue regular field monitoring
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants
Maintain consistent soil moisture with drip irrigation
Apply balanced fertilizers based on soil test recommendations
Scout for aphids and thrips that can vector viral diseases
Rotate crops each season to prevent soil-borne disease buildup

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of potato is caused by Alternaria solani. It is a common fungal disease that primarily affects older, stressed plants and can significantly reduce yield and quality.
symptoms: Dark brown to black circular spots with concentric rings (target/bull's-eye pattern) on older leaves
Lesions surrounded by yellow chlorotic halo
Lower leaves affected first; disease progresses upward
Tubers may show dark, sunken lesions with corky tissue underneath
Severe defoliation reduces tuber size and yield
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin fungicides at disease onset
Spray every 7–14 days depending on weather conditions
Begin applications when plants reach 6 inches tall or at first disease symptoms
Use systemic fungicides (strobilurin + triazole) for active infections
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed tubers
Plant early-maturing or resistant potato varieties
Rotate potatoes with non-solanaceous crops for at least 2–3 years
Avoid excessive nitrogen — maintain balanced fertility
Irrigate in the morning so foliage dries quickly

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight, caused by Phytophthora infestans, is the most devastating potato disease in history, responsible for the Irish Famine. It can destroy an entire field within days under favorable conditions.
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green to brown lesions on leaves, rapidly enlarging
White cottony mold on the underside of lesions during humid conditions
Affected tissue turns dark brown to black and the plant collapses
Brown to reddish internal rot of tubers with firm, granular texture
Foul-smelling rotting tubers in the field or in storage
treatment: Apply metalaxyl (Ridomil) or cymoxanil-based fungicides immediately upon detection
Use contact fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) as protective sprays
Destroy infected haulms (tops) 2–3 weeks before harvest to prevent tuber infection
Remove and destroy infected volunteer potato plants
Do not store infected tubers — they will rot and infect healthy ones
prevention: Plant certified disease-free, resistant potato varieties
Apply preventive fungicide program from early canopy closure
Avoid overhead irrigation; irrigate in the morning
Hill soil over potato rows to protect tubers from spore wash-down
Use decision support systems to time spray applications

Raspberry___healthy.description: The raspberry plant is healthy and shows no signs of disease or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue routine monitoring and maintenance
prevention: Prune out old floricanes after harvest to reduce disease pressure
Ensure adequate spacing for air circulation between canes
Avoid overhead watering — use drip or trickle irrigation
Scout for aphids which can vector raspberry mosaic virus
Apply dormant sprays to manage overwintering pest and disease inoculum

Soybean___healthy.description: The soybean plant appears healthy with no signs of disease, nutrient deficiency, or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue standard crop monitoring and management
prevention: Use certified, high-quality soybean seed with good germination
Practice crop rotation — avoid continuous soybean planting
Scout regularly for soybean aphid, spider mites, and stink bugs
Monitor for sudden death syndrome and white mold in susceptible fields
Apply seed treatments with fungicide and insecticide for early-season protection

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on squash is caused by Podosphaera xanthii. It is one of the most common diseases of cucurbits and can severely reduce yield and fruit quality.
symptoms: White to gray powdery coating on upper and lower leaf surfaces
Infected leaves turn yellow, then brown, and die prematurely
Stems and petioles may also be covered in white powder
Fruit may be undersized or sunburned due to loss of leaf cover
Severely affected plants produce fewer and smaller fruits
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicides or potassium bicarbonate at first sign of infection
Use triazole fungicides (myclobutanil, triadimefon) for systemic control
Neem oil or horticultural oil sprays can reduce disease spread
Spray plant thoroughly including undersides of leaves
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
prevention: Plant powdery mildew-resistant squash varieties
Avoid planting in areas with poor air movement
Space plants adequately for good airflow
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization that promotes succulent growth
Apply preventive sulfur sprays in areas with history of the disease

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch of strawberry is caused by Diplocarpon earlianum. It is a common fungal disease that reduces plant vigor and can significantly impact yield.
symptoms: Small, irregular, purple to dark brown spots scattered across leaf surface
Spots do not develop pale centers (distinguishing it from common leaf spot)
Severely infected leaves turn reddish-purple, then dry up and die
Crowns and petioles may also be infected in severe outbreaks
Overall plant appears scorched or dried out
treatment: Apply captan or myclobutanil fungicides at first sign of infection
Spray every 10–14 days during wet spring weather
Remove and destroy infected leaves during renovation
Use copper-based fungicides as an alternative organic treatment
prevention: Plant resistant strawberry varieties
Renovate strawberry beds promptly after harvest — mow and thin
Avoid overhead irrigation or water in the morning so leaves dry quickly
Maintain good plant spacing for air circulation
Apply protective fungicides in spring when new growth begins

Strawberry___healthy.description: The strawberry plant appears healthy with vibrant green leaves and no signs of disease or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue routine crop monitoring
prevention: Renovate beds after harvest to remove old leaves and reduce disease inoculum
Mulch with straw to keep fruit clean and reduce soil splash
Monitor for spider mites, strawberry weevil, and aphids
Maintain balanced irrigation to prevent drought and waterlogging stress
Replace beds every 3–4 years to avoid buildup of soil-borne pathogens

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of tomato is caused by Xanthomonas species. It affects leaves, stems, and fruit and is most severe during warm, rainy, or windy weather.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that become dark brown with yellow halos
Lesions may have a greasy or translucent appearance when held to light
Defoliation in severe cases exposes fruit to sunscald
Raised, dark, scabby lesions on fruit surface
Spots on stems appear as elongated, dark streaks
treatment: Apply copper hydroxide or copper sulfate bactericides preventively
Combine copper with mancozeb for improved efficacy
Spray on a 5–7 day schedule during wet or windy weather
Remove severely infected plants or leaves to reduce spread
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants and treated seeds
Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years
Use drip irrigation rather than overhead sprinklers
Plant resistant tomato varieties where available
Disinfect tools and equipment between plants

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of tomato is caused by Alternaria solani. It is a very common disease that affects the lower leaves of mature plants, reducing yield and fruit quality.
symptoms: Dark brown circular spots with distinctive concentric rings on older lower leaves
Yellow chlorotic halo surrounds each lesion
Lesions coalesce causing blighting and defoliation of lower canopy
Dark sunken lesions at the stem end of fruit (collar rot)
Stem lesions create dark, elongated cankers
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper fungicides at first symptom
Use systemic azoxystrobin or difenoconazole for advanced infections
Spray on 7–10 day schedule, more frequently in wet weather
Remove infected lower leaves to slow disease progression
Stake and cage plants to improve air circulation around foliage
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-host crops every 2–3 years
Use mulch to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation or water early in the day
Plant tomatoes in well-drained locations with good air movement
Apply preventive fungicide program from transplanting onward

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight on tomato is caused by Phytophthora infestans. It can destroy entire fields within a week under cool, moist conditions.
symptoms: Greasy, water-soaked lesions on leaves that quickly turn dark brown to black
White cottony mold on underside of lesions during humid conditions
Infected stems turn dark brown and collapse
Fruit develops large, brown, firm, greasy-looking rotted areas
Disease spreads rapidly through the entire plant and to neighboring plants
treatment: Apply metalaxyl-M (Ridomil Gold) or cymoxanil immediately at first sign
Use protective copper or chlorothalonil sprays as preventive cover
Remove and bag infected plants — do not compost
Spray neighboring plants immediately upon detection
Apply on a 5–7 day schedule during cool, wet weather
prevention: Monitor weather forecasts — cool nights + warm humid days trigger outbreaks
Apply preventive fungicides before infection periods
Avoid overhead irrigation and water in the morning
Destroy volunteer tomato and potato plants that harbor the pathogen
Plant late blight-tolerant varieties where available

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Tomato leaf mold is caused by Passalora fulva. It is primarily a disease of greenhouse tomatoes but can occur in field crops during humid conditions.
symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to gray-brown velvety mold on underside of leaves corresponding to upper spots
Infected leaves curl upward and turn yellow, then brown
Severe infections cause defoliation starting from lower canopy
Fruit infections are rare but can cause dark, leathery rot at the stem end
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, copper-based fungicides, or mancozeb at first signs
Use systemic fungicides (azoxystrobin, difenoconazole) for better control
Reduce humidity in greenhouse settings by increasing ventilation
Remove and destroy infected leaves to reduce spore load
Apply fungicide on 7–10 day intervals while conditions remain humid
prevention: Maintain relative humidity below 85% in greenhouses through ventilation
Space plants adequately for good air circulation
Use resistant tomato varieties (many modern greenhouse varieties have Cf resistance genes)
Avoid wetting foliage when watering
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops between seasons

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by Septoria lycopersici. It is one of the most common tomato foliar diseases, appearing soon after fruit set and causing extensive defoliation.
symptoms: Numerous small, circular spots with dark brown margins and pale gray-white centers
Tiny black specks (pycnidia) visible in the center of spots
Spots appear first on lower, older leaves
Rapid yellowing and defoliation from the bottom of the plant upward
Stems may also be infected in severe cases
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper fungicides at first sign
Spray every 7–10 days; increase frequency during wet weather
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves promptly
Systemic fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole) are effective for advanced cases
Ensure thorough spray coverage of lower leaf surfaces
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-host crops for 2 years minimum
Use mulch (straw, plastic) to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves
Stake and support tomato plants to lift foliage off the ground
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at the base in the morning
Remove and destroy tomato debris at end of season

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae) is a major pest of tomatoes. It thrives in hot, dry conditions and can rapidly build up to damaging populations.
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white stippling (dots) on upper leaf surface from feeding punctures
Leaves develop a bronze, silvery, or bleached appearance
Fine webbing on undersides of leaves and between stems in heavy infestations
Leaves turn yellow-brown and drop prematurely
Severe infestations cause plant defoliation and fruit sunscald
treatment: Apply abamectin, bifenazate, or spiromesifen miticides at first detection
Use horticultural oils or insecticidal soaps for organic control
Rotate between miticide classes to prevent resistance development
Spray undersides of leaves where mites feed and reproduce
Increase irrigation — mites thrive in dry, dusty conditions
prevention: Scout plants weekly especially during hot, dry weather
Use overhead irrigation or water plants to reduce dust and mite buildup
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill beneficial predatory mites
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) in greenhouse settings
Remove and destroy heavily infested plant material promptly

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot of tomato is caused by Corynespora cassiicola. It is an emerging disease increasingly reported in field and greenhouse tomatoes worldwide.
symptoms: Brown circular to irregular spots with concentric rings resembling a target
Lesions have a yellow halo and dark brown border
Spots appear on leaves, stems, and fruit
Fruit lesions are sunken, dark, and may lead to secondary fungal rots
Defoliation under heavy disease pressure reduces yield significantly
treatment: Apply azoxystrobin, chlorothalonil, or fluxapyroxad fungicides at disease onset
Spray on 7–14 day intervals depending on weather conditions
Combine contact and systemic fungicides for best results
Remove heavily infected plant parts to slow disease spread
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops
Use mulch to reduce soil splash
Maintain adequate plant spacing for air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen which can increase susceptibility
Monitor closely in warm, humid conditions (25–30°C) which favor the disease

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is a devastating viral disease transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). It causes severe yield losses in tomato-growing regions worldwide.
symptoms: Upward curling and cupping of young leaves
Yellowing (chlorosis) of leaf margins and interveinal areas
Stunted plant growth and reduced leaf size
Flower drop and poor fruit set leading to drastically reduced yield
Infected plants have a characteristic bushy, stunted appearance
treatment: No cure for infected plants — focus on whitefly vector control
Apply imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, or spirotetramat to control whitefly populations
Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent virus spread
Use reflective mulches to repel whitefly from young transplants
Apply insecticides at transplanting and continue on a scheduled program
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties (most modern commercial varieties carry Ty genes)
Use insect-proof mesh screens in greenhouse production
Apply systemic insecticide at transplanting for early whitefly control
Remove infected and volunteer plants from field and surrounding areas
Avoid planting near other solanaceous crops that may harbor whitefly

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) is a highly stable mechanically transmitted virus. It is easily spread by contact with infected plants, tools, and hands, and can persist in soil and plant debris for long periods.
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green patches on leaves
Leaf distortion, curling, or fern-like appearance of leaflets (fernleaf)
Stunted plant growth and reduced leaf size
Yellow streaking or mottling on fruit
Fruit may be misshapen with internal browning in some strains
treatment: No chemical cure is available for viral infections
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to prevent spread
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach solution between plants
Wash hands thoroughly before and after handling plants
Control aphids and other potential secondary vectors
prevention: Plant certified virus-free seeds or transplants
Use ToMV-resistant tomato varieties (resistance gene Tm-2)
Disinfect tools, stakes, and equipment before use
Do not use tobacco products while handling tomato plants
Wash hands before entering the field or greenhouse

Tomato___healthy.description: The tomato plant appears healthy with vibrant green foliage and no visible signs of disease, virus, or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required
Continue regular monitoring and preventive care
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops each season
Apply preventive fungicide program starting at transplanting
Use mulch to reduce soil splash and conserve moisture
Scout weekly for early signs of diseases and pests
Maintain balanced fertilization based on soil test results
